metadata.subject: Data Structures (CS1101)
metadata.school: Faculty of Computer Science, Alexandru Ioan Cuza University of Iasi, Romania

curricular_context.curriculum_area: Computer Science - Data Structures and Algorithms
curricular_context.national_or_institutional_curriculum_ref: Faculty of Computer Science CS1101 programme specification, UAIC 2018/2019
curricular_context.program_of_study_reference: Bachelor of Computer Science, Year 1, Mandatory Course
curricular_context.school_profile: Alexandru Ioan Cuza University, Faculty of Computer Science, Iasi, Romania
curricular_context.student_profile: First-year students with basic programming knowledge in C; background in high-school mathematics including sets, functions, and elementary combinatorics
curricular_context.assessment_policy_reference: Faculty of Computer Science examination regulations; continuous assessment (50%) + final exam (50%)

curriculum_vision.general_description: This one-year course develops students' ability to design, analyse, and implement fundamental data structures and algorithms in C. Starting from the notion of an algorithm and progressing through linear structures, trees, graphs, sorting, searching, hashing, and string structures, students build both theoretical understanding and practical programming skill.
curriculum_vision.rationale: Data structures form the backbone of every non-trivial software system. A computer science graduate who cannot reason about algorithmic complexity and choose the right data structure for a given problem will consistently produce slow, unmaintainable code. This course provides the conceptual vocabulary and practical tools to avoid those pitfalls.
big_ideas: The right data structure transforms an intractable problem into a tractable one.
Asymptotic analysis lets us reason about performance without running code.
Recursive structure enables recursive algorithms — trees, graphs, and divide-and-conquer all share this principle.
Abstract Data Types separate interface from implementation, enabling modular design.
Real-world systems (search engines, databases, operating systems) are built on the structures studied in this course.
essential_questions: How do we measure whether an algorithm is efficient?
When does the choice of data structure determine whether a solution is feasible?
What trade-offs exist between different implementations of the same ADT?
How do recursive algorithms inherit the structure of the data they operate on?
How are the data structures in this course used in the software you use every day?
enduring_understandings: Asymptotic notation captures growth rate, not absolute running time.
Every ADT has multiple implementations with different performance profiles.
Trees and graphs are recursive structures best understood and manipulated recursively.
Sorting and searching are ubiquitous sub-problems whose optimal solutions depend on context.
Hashing offers O(1) average-case performance by exploiting randomness and uniform distribution.
curriculum_vision.real_world_relevance: Every piece of software students will build in their career — web services, databases, compilers, operating systems, machine-learning pipelines — relies on data structures covered in this course: hash maps for symbol tables, heaps for priority queues, graphs for dependency resolution, tries for autocomplete, B-trees for database indices.
graduate_profile_connections: Apply computational thinking to decompose and solve complex problems
Design and implement efficient, correct software systems
Communicate algorithmic reasoning clearly in both written and oral form
Engage in lifelong learning as algorithms and data structures continue to evolve

core_domains.description: This unit introduces the notion of algorithm, pseudocode, elementary data types, and one-dimensional arrays. Students learn to express solutions as step-by-step procedures and understand how data is represented in memory.

Students learn to measure algorithm efficiency using asymptotic notation (O, Omega, Theta) and analyse recursive algorithms by solving recurrence relations via substitution, iteration, recurrence-tree, and Master Theorem methods.
This unit covers ADTs for linear collections: linear lists (ordered and unordered), stacks, and queues. Both array-based and linked-list implementations are presented.
Trees are introduced as a recursive data structure. The unit covers binary trees, traversal algorithms, priority queues (max-heaps), and disjoint set collections (union-find).

Graphs are introduced as a general model for pairwise relationships. The unit covers undirected and directed graphs, adjacency-matrix and adjacency-list representations, DFS, BFS, and algorithms for connected/strongly connected components.

This unit surveys major comparison-based sorting algorithms (bubble, insertion, selection, heap, merge, quick sort) and distribution-based algorithms (counting sort, radix sort). The O(n log n) lower bound for comparison-based sorting is discussed.

The unit addresses the search problem in static (binary search on sorted array) and dynamic (BST) settings. AVL trees and red-black trees are introduced as self-balancing BSTs that guarantee O(log n) worst-case operations.
core_concepts: The binary search: the static aspect
The binary tree associated to the binary search

core_domains.description: Hash tables are presented as a practical solution to the dictionary problem. The unit covers direct-address tables, hash functions (division and multiplication methods), and collision resolution via chaining and open addressing.
The unit covers trie-based data structures for string keys: standard tries, Patricia (compressed) tries, and suffix trees, enabling O(m) worst-case string search where m is the key length.

concept_progression_overview.description: Algorithm definition, pseudocode, elementary data types, arrays, and asymptotic analysis

concept_progression_overview.phase: Phase 2 - Linear and Hierarchical Structures (Semester 1, Units 3-4)
concept_progression_overview.description: Linear lists, stacks, queues, trees, heaps, and union-find

concept_progression_overview.phase: Phase 3 - Graph Theory and Sorting (Semester 2, Units 5-6)
concept_progression_overview.description: Graphs, DFS/BFS, connected components, and comparison/distribution-based sorting

concept_progression_overview.phase: Phase 4 - Search, Hashing, and Strings (Semester 2, Units 7-9)
concept_progression_overview.description: Binary search, balanced BSTs, hash tables, tries, and suffix trees

semester_goals: Define and explain: algorithm, pseudocode, variable, data type, array

semester_plan.title: Semester 2
semester_goals: Define and explain: graph, vertex, edge, digraph, adjacency matrix
Describe the algorithmic complexity of each operation covered
State the trade-offs between implementations discussed
Define and explain: sorting, in-place, stable, comparison-based, bubble sort

learning_objectives: Define and explain: algorithm, pseudocode, variable, data type, array
Describe the algorithmic complexity of each operation covered
State the trade-offs between implementations discussed
big_ideas: Algorithms are precise, unambiguous instructions for solving a class of problems.
The choice of data representation directly affects algorithm design.

learning_objectives: Define and explain: time complexity, space complexity, Big-O, Omega, Theta
Describe the algorithmic complexity of each operation covered
State the trade-offs between implementations discussed
big_ideas: The asymptotic growth rate of an algorithm is more important than its absolute running time.
Recursive algorithms can be analysed using recurrence relations.

learning_objectives: Define and explain: ADT, linear list, linked list, node, pointer
Describe the algorithmic complexity of each operation covered
State the trade-offs between implementations discussed
big_ideas: An ADT separates the interface from the implementation.
Array implementations offer O(1) access but rigid capacity; linked lists offer dynamic size at the cost of O(n) access.

learning_objectives: Define and explain: tree, binary tree, root, leaf, height
Describe the algorithmic complexity of each operation covered
State the trade-offs between implementations discussed
big_ideas: Recursive structure enables recursive algorithms.
The heap property gives O(log n) insert/extract-max.

learning_objectives: Define and explain: graph, vertex, edge, digraph, adjacency matrix
Describe the algorithmic complexity of each operation covered
State the trade-offs between implementations discussed
big_ideas: A graph G=(V,E) is a flexible model for any binary relationship.
The choice of representation (matrix vs list) trades memory for access speed.

learning_objectives: Define and explain: sorting, in-place, stable, comparison-based, bubble sort
Describe the algorithmic complexity of each operation covered
State the trade-offs between implementations discussed
big_ideas: No comparison-based sort can beat Omega(n log n) in the worst case.
Divide-and-conquer yields O(n log n) algorithms.

key_concepts: The binary search: the static aspect
The binary tree associated to the binary search
learning_objectives: Define and explain: binary search, binary search tree, BST, in-order successor, rotation
Describe the algorithmic complexity of each operation covered
State the trade-offs between implementations discussed
big_ideas: Binary search exploits sorted order to achieve O(log n) search.
A BST dynamically maintains sorted order but degenerates to O(n) without balancing.

learning_objectives: Define and explain: hash table, hash function, collision, chaining, open addressing
Describe the algorithmic complexity of each operation covered
State the trade-offs between implementations discussed
big_ideas: A hash function maps arbitrary keys to a fixed-size table enabling O(1) average lookup.
Collisions are inevitable and must be resolved; the choice of strategy affects load tolerance.

learning_objectives: Define and explain: trie, prefix tree, Patricia trie, compressed trie, suffix tree
Describe the algorithmic complexity of each operation covered
State the trade-offs between implementations discussed
big_ideas: A trie stores strings by sharing common prefixes, giving O(m) operations independent of n.
Compressing single-child chains yields Patricia tries, dramatically reducing space.

assessment_framework.philosophy: Assessment is continuous and multi-modal. Formative assessment informs daily instruction; summative assessment evaluates mastery at the end of each unit. Students are encouraged to self-assess and engage in peer review to develop metacognitive awareness.

diagnostic_assessment.purpose: Identify entry-level gaps before each unit and at course start
methods: Entry quiz at first lesson of each unit

formative_assessment.purpose: Monitor understanding during each lesson and adjust instruction
methods: Exit tickets at end of lecture
formative_assessment.feedback_strategy: Immediate verbal feedback in class; written feedback on exit tickets returned at the next session

summative_assessment.purpose: Evaluate mastery of unit and course learning outcomes
tools: Automated test cases for C programs
grading_criteria: Correctness of implemented data structure or algorithm
Appropriate use of the data structure for the problem
Correct asymptotic complexity analysis with justification
Clean, well-structured, and commented C code
Written exam: accuracy of definitions, complexity derivations, and algorithm traces

methods: Reflective journal entry after each lecture
prompts: Can I define the key vocabulary of this unit without notes?
Can I trace through the main algorithms of this unit on a new example?
Can I identify when to apply each data structure covered?
What concept am I least confident about, and what will I do about it?
tools: Printed self-check checklist distributed at end of each unit

methods: Peer code review of C implementations

teaching_and_learning_strategy.overall_instructional_approach: Constructivist: students build understanding incrementally through concrete examples, algorithm tracing, and coding practice before abstract generalisations are introduced.
teaching_and_learning_strategy.inquiry_based_learning: Each unit opens with a motivating problem that the new data structure is shown to solve efficiently.
teaching_and_learning_strategy.differentiation_strategy: Tiered tasks: all students complete core exercises; advanced students complete extension proofs and complexity analyses.
technology_integration: GCC compiler + IDE for lab sessions
Online data structure visualisers for self-study
LMS for assignment submission and feedback

support_for_struggling_students: Simpler worked examples with smaller inputs
Reference sheet with key definitions and complexity tables
extensions_for_advanced_students: Analyse a more complex variant of the covered algorithm
Compare the covered approach with an alternative from the literature
Prove a stated property or complexity bound rigorously
adaptations_for_special_needs: Provide printed slides before the lecture
language_support: Key vocabulary list in both English and Romanian
Bilingual lecture slides available on request
accessibility_considerations: Use high-contrast colours on the whiteboard
Provide digital versions of all handouts

textbooks_or_references: Madalina Raschip, Cristian Gatu - CS1101 Data Structures Lecture Notes
T.H. Cormen, C.E. Leiserson, R.L. Rivest, C. Stein - Introduction to Algorithms (CLRS), 3rd ed.
D. Knuth - The Art of Computer Programming, Vol. 1-3
R. Sedgewick, K. Wayne - Algorithms, 4th ed.
manipulatives_or_physical_materials: Index cards for tracing pointer-based structures
multimedia_resources: Lecture slide PDFs for all 12 lectures
Online data structure visualisers (VisuAlgo, CS Academy)
teacher_preparation_materials: Prepare 2-3 worked examples per lesson with step-by-step solutions before each lecture
Review student exit tickets after each lesson and adjust the next lesson accordingly
Prepare extension problems for advanced students for each unit
Maintain a shared code repository with sample C implementations for student reference

milestones.evidence: Mid-term lab assignment on Sorting and Searching
Final exam + capstone programming assignment covering all units

project_links: Discrete Mathematics: apply graph theory and combinatorics concepts
Computer Architecture: relate data structure memory layouts to cache behaviour
Databases: explain how B-trees and hash joins are used in relational databases
Compilers: demonstrate how expression trees and symbol-table hashing are used
Operating Systems: show how heaps (schedulers) and hash tables (page tables) are used

recommended_activities: Attend a workshop on active learning strategies for algorithms courses
Review recent CLRS errata and supplement lecture notes accordingly
Participate in peer observation: invite a colleague to observe one lecture per semester
Reflect on assessment data at end of each unit to inform next delivery
Explore automated grading tools for C programming assignments
professional_development.reflection_schedule: After each unit: record what worked, what did not, and what to adjust next year
professional_development.peer_collaboration: Share end-of-unit reflection notes with a teaching colleague; discuss improvements
professional_development.research_connections: Stay current with CLRS updates and ACM SIGCSE conference proceedings on CS1 pedagogy

optional_practice: Solve additional exercises from CLRS relevant to Foundations
Experiment with edge cases in your C implementation
Solve additional exercises from CLRS relevant to Algorithm Analysis
Solve additional exercises from CLRS relevant to Linear Data Structures
Solve additional exercises from CLRS relevant to Trees
Solve additional exercises from CLRS relevant to Graphs
extension_tasks: Research an advanced variant of the covered data structure
Read and summarise a Wikipedia article on an application of the unit topic
homework_and_extension.year_project: Capstone programming project: implement a mini-application (e.g., a simple spell-checker using a trie and hash table) that demonstrates mastery of at least three different data structures covered during the year.

year_end_review_activities: Full-year concept map: students collaboratively map all 9 units and their interconnections
Exam preparation: guided review of past exam questions covering all units
Student presentations: each student presents their capstone project (10 minutes)
synthesis_tasks: Core concept covered: Example 1. Sum of the first n natural numbers
final_reflection_prompts: Which data structure surprised you the most, and why?
Describe a real-world software system you now understand better because of this course.
What would you implement differently if you could redo your first unit assignment?
What topics from this course are you most curious to explore further?
curriculum_closure.transition_to_next_year: Students who complete CS1101 are prepared for Algorithm Design and Analysis (CS2101), Databases (CS3201), and Operating Systems (CS2202), all of which assume fluency with the data structures covered in this course.

additional_resources: VisuAlgo - Online algorithm and data structure visualiser (visualgo.net)
